title: Creating a Large Text File Viewer by Vibe Coding with Visual Studio Code, Cline, OpenRouter, and Claude 3.7
summary: I just created another Windows 10/11 application using AI. This is a follow-up to the SquareCap program I posted about a few weeks ago .   The problem I was trying to solve this time was opening and searching extremely large text files.   I used to use the old Mandiant Highlighter program for this, but it was last updated in 2011 and couldn't handle the 26 GB text file I wanted to open.   If you're wondering what that file is, it's a dump of the contents of the main Starfield.esm file from the Bethesda Game Studios game called Starfield. I use the xdump64 program bundled with xEdit.   You can try this program for yourself if you like. It's a stand-alone Windows C# .NET 9 application that runs on Windows 10 and 11.  Like my last program, all I did was work with the model for about 3 hours to get it to where it is now.   I tried for an hour or so to implement a "highlight all search matches" function but could not get that to work.    The screen capture cuts of the right side of the page where the search and match feature lives. You can download the .exe from GitHub at https://github.com/taosecurity/BejView .   Windows will probably complain because it is not signed.  As with my last program, I have no idea of code quality or vulnerabilities. This was a fun exercise to see if I could create a program that would address a problem I was working. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Creating a Windows Application Using Visual Studio Code, Cline, OpenRouter, and Claude
summary: I just created a Windows 10/11 application that takes square screen captures. I did zero coding myself but used Visual Studio Code, Cline, OpenRouter, and Claude.   I got the idea by watching a video on so-called Vibe programming by a YouTuber named Memory . I have zero Windows programming experience although I have recently been playing with simple video game development.   After creating the application I was able to use Cline to help me commit it to GitHub. You can find it at https://github.com/taosecurity/SquareSnap/ . Note that if you download the .exe Windows will complain because it is unsigned. If you worry about back doors just look at the code yourself -- or have your own LLM read it!   This was just an experiment to see how these tools work. I decided to try creating a screen capture program that only takes captures in a square or 1:1 format because it's useful for social media, and especially YouTube posts.   I had not found anything prior to this. This is really amazing but I obviously cannot comment on code quality. I'm happy that it's working well enough to take the screen caps in this blog post. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Happy 22nd Birthday TaoSecurity Blog
summary: Happy birthday TaoSecurity Blog, born on this day in 2003! The best way to digest the key lessons from this site is to browse my four volume Best of TaoSecurity Blog book series , published in 2020. It's available in print as seen here, or as a properly formatted HTML-based digital book -- none of that PDF-based fixed format nonsense. title: What Are Normal Users Supposed to Do with IDS Alerts from Network Gear?
summary: Probably once a week, I see posts like this in the r/Ubiquiti subreddit. Ubiquiti makes network gear that includes an "IDS/IPS" feature. I own some older Ubiquiti gear so I am familiar with the product. When you enable this feature, you get alerts like this one, posted by a Redditor: This is everything you get from Ubiquiti.   The Redditor is concerned that their system may be trying to compromise someone on the Internet. This is my answer to how to handle these alerts.   == This is another example of this sort of alert being almost worthless for most users. The key is trying to understand what COULD have caused the alert to trigger. CVEs, whatever, are irrelevant at this point. Here is one way to get SOME idea of what is happening. Go to https://rules.emergingthreats.net/open/suricata-7.0.3/rules/ Download the file that is named as the first part of the alert. Here that is EXPLOIT. https://rules.emergingthreats.net/open/suricata-7.0.3/rules/emerging-exploit.rules Find the rule that fired. This can take some digging. Here is what I ended up doing. grep -i possible emerging-exploit.rules | grep -i log4j | grep -i obfuscation | grep -i udp | grep -i outbound Here it is. alert udp $HOME_NET any -> any any (msg:"ET EXPLOIT Possible Apache log4j RCE Attempt - 2021/12/12 Obfuscation Observed M2 (udp) (Outbound) (CVE-2021-44228)"; content:"|24 7b|"; content:"|24 7b 3a 3a|"; within:100; fast_pattern; reference:cve,2021-44228; classtype:attempted-admin; sid:2034805; rev:3; metadata:attack_target Server, created_at 2021_12_18, cve CVE_2021_44228, deployment Perimeter, deployment Internal, signature_severity Major, tag Exploit, updated_at 2023_06_05, mitre_tactic_id TA0001, mitre_tactic_name Initial_Access, mitre_technique_id T1190, mitre_technique_name Exploit_Public_Facing_Application;) You can ignore 90% of this. The key is here: content:"|24 7b|"; content:"|24 7b 3a 3a|"; within:100 and here: udp $HOME_NET any -> any any Now, you have to guess how likely it might be there you could have ANY UDP traffic from your home network to anywhere, on any ports, that contain this string 24 7b followed by this string 24 7b 3a 3a within the next 100 bytes? I'm guessing there's a decent chance that could happen in random, normal traffic. Therefore, without any other evidence, I think you can ignore this alert. If you want to have a better chance at understanding this in the future, please feel free to check out anything I've written about network security monitoring. Good luck!   == This problem is why I have promoted network security monitoring since 1998 and subtitled my first book "Beyond Intrusion Detection." Network intrusion detection, by itself, with no supporting data and without even rule explanations, is almost worthless. Thankfully in this case the vendor is at least using an open rule set, enabling this feeble exploration. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: My First Book Is 20 Years Old Today
summary: On this day in 2004, Addison-Wesley/Pearson published my first book, The Tao of Network Security Monitoring: Beyond Intrusion Detection . This post from 2017 explains the differences between my first four books and why I wrote Tao .  Today, I'm always thrilled when I hear that someone found my books useful.  I am done writing books on security, but I believe the core tactics and strategies in all my books are still relevant. I'm not sure that's a good thing, though. I would have liked to not need the tactics and strategies in my book anymore. "The Cloud," along with so many other developments and approaches, was supposed to have saved us by now. Consider this statement from a report describing CISA’s red team against a fed agency:  “[A]ttempts to capture forensic data via packet captures occurred directly on the compromised Solaris and Windows hosts, where the red team observed the data being collected and therefore had the opportunity to disrupt collection, tamper with evidence files, and better adapt and evade their defenses.” This is why you should not rely on EDR, either, for your only understanding of adversary activity. The adversary can shut down or alter your endpoint security tooling. For network security monitoring, you also shouldn’t collect on endpoints. Collect using network taps, or in a pinch, span ports. There is nothing in this intrusion that would have been a surprise in 2004. Here is the post I published in 2004 when the first copy showed up on my doorstep .    There's nothing like getting a real copy in your hands, and I cherish that experience! I will probably revisit this event in 5 years. See you then! Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Retrieving Deleted Files on the Commodore C64 in 1987
summary: When I was a sophomore in high school, from 1987 to 1988, my friend Paul and I had Commodore C64 computers. There was a new graphical user interface called GEOS that had transformed the way we interacted with our computers. We used the C64 to play games but also write papers for school. One day Paul called me. He was clearly troubled. He had somehow dragged his newly completed term paper into the trash bin instead of the printer. If I recall correctly, back then they were right next to each other (although the screen shot above shows them separate).  Paul asked if I knew any tricks that could retrieve his paper. There was no undelete function in GEOS. I subscribed to a magazine called Compute's Gazette, for Commodore owners. I remembered seeing an article in the magazine that included code for undeleting files dropped in the GEOS "Waste Basket." All I had to do was type it in by hand, save it to a 5 1/4 inch floppy, drive to Paul's house, and see if the program would recover his paper. Thankfully it all worked out, and we did recover his work! Almost 37 years later, I found that issue of Compute's Gazette in the fabulous Internet Archive. It's in issue 54, dated December 1987 . I had to find it by manually looking at covers. The index doesn't list it by name. Here is the article explaining the reason for the program. It's interesting to think that the C64 handled deleted files the same way later operating systems did, enabling digital drive forensics. Here's the entire program, in BASIC: The magazine offered tips on entering programs manually. There's even a "proofreader" to check syntax. Of course, you have to enter that in yourself too! These days you can download the program from the Internet . I was really pleased to find this program after so many years. It appears the magazine published an update version in a later issue, but I'm pretty sure I did not use that one.  Incidentally, I sold my entire Commodore collection on eBay several years ago, so these are only memories. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: My Last Email with W. Richard Stevens
summary: In the fall of 1998 I joined the AFCERT. I became acquainted with the amazing book TCP/IP Illustrated, Volume 1: The Protocols  by W. Richard Stevens. About a year later I exchanged emails with Mr. Stevens. Here is the last exchange, as forwarded from my AFCERT email address to my home email. From "Capt Richard Bejtlich - Real Time Chief" Mon Sep  6 18:27:35 1999 X-Mozilla-Keys:                                                                                  Received: from kinda.csap.af.mil (kinda.csap.af.mil [192.203.2.250])           by mw4.texas.net (2.4/2.4) with SMTP   id RAA22116 for <bejtlich@texas.net>; Mon, 6 Sep 1999 17:27:38 -0500 (CDT) Received: by kinda.csap.af.mil (Smail3.1.29.1 #7) id m11O7Ee-000NcwC; Mon, 6 Sep 99 17:27 CDT Received: from walt.ip.af.mil(192.168.1.142) by kinda via smap (V1.3) id sma014865; Mon Sep  6 17:27:36 1999 Received: from kinda.csap.af.mil by walt.ip.af.mil with smtp (Smail3.1.29.1 #6) id m11O7Ed-000VruC; Mon, 6 Sep 99 22:27 GMT Sender: bejtlich Message-ID: <37D43FD7.52CC675A@kinda.csap.af.mil> Date: Mon, 06 Sep 1999 22:27:35 +0000 From: Capt Richard Bejtlich - Real Time Chief   <richard.bejtlich@kinda.csap.af.mil> Organization: AFCERT X-Mailer: Mozilla 4.6 [en] (X11; U; SunOS 5.6 sun4u) X-Accept-Language: en MIME-Version: 1.0 To: bejtlich@texas.net Subject: [Fwd: Re: TCP/IP Illustrated Vol I 2nd ed?] Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit Content-Type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii X-UIDL: 7b89a44b661b28334c3553b3b92998bd -------- Original Message -------- From: rstevens@kohala.com (W. Richard Stevens) Subject: Re: TCP/IP Illustrated Vol I 2nd ed? To: Capt Richard Bejtlich - Real Time Chief <richard.bejtlich@kinda.csap.af.mil> [In your message of Aug 18,  2:37pm you write:] >  > Your books are excellent and everyone in my office relies upon them!   Many thanks.   > Do you plan on writing a second edition of TCP/IP Illustrated Vol 1 (The > Protocols)?  I see you have written second editions of UNIX Network > Programming, and I'm wondering if the TCP/IP books are next. Actually, I am first working on a revision of my APUE book, then it will be time for TCP Vol. 1.  I am hesitant to start on TCP Vol. 1 too soon, given the "uncertain" status of IPv6 at the present.  I keep hoping things will settle down with IPv6 and vendors will start shipping real implementations. Rich Stevens Mr. Stevens died a couple weeks after this email in a car accident at the age of 48 . RIP Mr. Stevens and thank you for your work. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Key Network Questions
summary: I wrote this on 7 December 2018 but never published it until today. The following are the "key network questions" which "would answer many key questions about [a] network, without having to access a third party log repository. This data is derived from mining Zeek log data as it is created, rather than storing and querying Zeek logs in a third party repository." This is how I was thinking about Zeek data in the second half of 2018. 1. What networking technologies are in use, over user-specified intervals?    1. Enumerate non-IP protocols (IPv6, unusual Ethertypes)    2. Enumerate IPv4 and IPv6 protocols (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.)    3. What is the local IP network topology/addressing scheme? 2. What systems are providing core services to the network, over user-specified intervals?    1. DHCP    2. DNS    3. NTP    4. Domain Controller    5. File sharing    6. Default gateway (via DHCP inspection, other?)    7. Web and cloud services 3. What tunnel mechanisms are in use, over user-specified intervals?    1. IPSec or other VPNs    2. SOCKS proxy    3. Web proxy (port 3128)    4. Other proxy 4. What access services are in use, over user-specified intervals?    1. SSH    2. Telnet    3. RDP    4. VNC    5. SMB    6. Other 5. What file transfer services are in use, over user-specified intervals?    1. SCP or other SSH-enabled file transfers    2. FTP    3. SMB    4. NFS 6. Encryption measurement, over user-specified intervals    1. What encryption methods are in use?    2. What percentage of network traffic over a user-specified interval is encrypted, and by which method? 7. Bandwidth measurement, over user-specified intervals    1. Aggregate    2. By IP address    3. By service 8. Conversation tracking, over user-specified intervals    1. Top N connection pairs    2. Bottom N connection pairs 9. Detection counts, over user-specified intervals    1. Provide a counter of messages from Zeek weird.log    2. Provide a counter of messages from other Zeek detection logs 10. For each IP address (or possibly IP-MAC address pairing), over user-specified intervals, build a profile with the following:    1. First seen, last seen    2. Observed names via DNS, SMB, other    3. Core services accessed and provided    4. Tunnel mechanisms used and provided    5. Access services used and provided    6. File transfer services used and provided    7. Encryption methods    8. Bandwidth measurements    9. Top N and bottom N conversation tracking    10. Detection counts Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Cybersecurity Is a Social, Policy, and Wicked Problem
summary: Cybersecurity is a social and policy problem, not a scientific or technical problem. Cybersecurity is also a wicked problem. In a landmark 1973 article, Dilemmas in a General Theory of Planning , urban planners Horst W. J. Rittel and Melvin M. Webber described wicked problems in these terms: “The search for scientific bases for confronting problems of social policy is bound to fail, because of the nature of these problems. They are ‘wicked’ problems, whereas science has developed to deal with ‘tame’ problems. Policy problems cannot be definitively described. Moreover, in a pluralistic society there is nothing like the undisputable public good; there is no objective definition of equity; policies that respond to social problems cannot be meaningfully correct or false; and it makes no sense to talk about ‘optimal solutions’ to social problems unless severe qualifications are imposed first. Even worse, there are no ‘solutions’ in the sense of definitive and objective answers.” Other wicked problems include climate change, smuggling, and nuclear weaponry.  There is no “perfect new normal” because there is no “solution” for cybersecurity.  To quote Marcus Ranum from the September 2007 issue of Information Security Magazine : “Will the future be more secure? It'll be just as insecure as it possibly can, while still continuing to function. Just like it is today.”  A report by the Australian government titled Tackling Wicked Problems: A Public Policy Perspective suggests that there are three strategies for mitigating wicked problems: authoritative, competitive, and collaborative. Similarly, cybersecurity will likely require some combination of all three. In summary, my modest new normal is this: anyone commenting on cybersecurity will recognize that it is a wicked problem that cannot be “solved,” but it may be mitigated, over decades, using expertise and approaches from multiple disciplines, least among them technical acumen. If pressed to provide a technical element of the new normal, I offer “building visibility in” as one tenet. Asset owners need to understand how their digital resources are used and abused, and anyone providing computing resources should include the logging and access needed to do so. * I found this note dated 1 June 2020 on my hard drive and decided to publish it today. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Core Writing Word and Page Counts
summary: I want to make a note of the numbers of words and pages in my core security writings. The Tao of Network Security Monitoring / 236k words / 833 pages Extrusion Detection / 113k words / 417 pages The Practice of Network Security Monitoring / 97k words / 380 pages The Best of TaoSecurity Blog, Vol 1 / 84k words / 357 pages The Best of TaoSecurity Blog, Vol 2 / 96k words / 429 pages The Best of TaoSecurity Blog, Vol 3 / 89k words / 485 pages The Best of TaoSecurity Blog, Vol 4 / 96k words / 429 pages The total is 811k words and 3,330 pages. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Happy 20th Birthday TaoSecurity Blog
summary: Happy 20th birthday TaoSecurity Blog , born on 8 January 2003 .  Thank you Blogger Blogger (now part of Google) has continuously hosted this blog for 20 years, for free. I'd like to thank Blogger and Google for providing this platform for two decades. It's tough to find extant self-hosted security content that was born at the same time, or earlier. Bruce Schneier's Schneier on Security is the main one that comes to mind. If not for the wonderful Internet Archive , many blogs from the early days would be lost. Statistics In my 15 year post I included some statistics, so here are a few, current as of the evening of 7 January: I think it's cool to see almost 29 million "all time" views, but that's not the whole story. Here are the so-called "all time" statistics: It turns out that Blogger only started capturing these numbers in January 2011. That means I've had almost 29 million views in the last 12 years.  I don't know what happened on 20 April 2022, when I had almost 1.5 million views? Top Ten Posts Since January 2011 Here are the top ten all time posts: I'm really pleased to see posts like  Security and the One Percent: A Thought Exercise in Estimation and Consequences and  Digital Offense Capabilities Are Currently Net Negative for the Security Ecosystem  in this list. We've had some discussion on these topics since I posted them in the fall of 2020, but not enough. The 99% continue to suffer at the hands of adversaries and those in the security 1% who ignore them. The Monetization Experiment I ran an advertising experiment from April 2021 through December 2022. I "earned" $116.96 by February 2022 and $104.39 by December 2022. I don't have view numbers for that whole period, but for calendar year 2022 I attracted a little over 7.5 million views. You can see that I earned about 1.4 x 10^-5 dollars per view. I disabled ads at the end of December. From Twitter to Mastodon One big change I can discern since my 15 year post is that I have now abandoned Twitter and migrated to Mastodon. You can find me at infosec.exchange/@taosecurity . My current Twitter follower count is about 59.7k, down from just over 60k. My current Mastodon follower count is 1.9k. I don't really care about followers, but I figured I would capture these numbers to see if there is any change in the next five years. The Latest Books I spent the early years of the pandemic collecting my 3,000 or so favorite blog posts into a four volume set called The Best of TaoSecurity Blog . I'm really pleased with these books, available via Amazon in print or digital format. They include original posts, but each receives commentary with modern thoughts on the original content. The fourth volume includes material not found in the blog, such as unpublished writings from my abandoned War Studies PhD program or Congressional testimonies. It looks like Amazon is randomly running a promotion on volume 2 of The Best of TaoSecurity Blog  while I am writing this post. The print edition is regularly $19.95, but it's currently priced at $7.89. I don't know how long it will last, but if you're interested please check it out.  I also co-wrote and published a book on stretching with a subject matter expert --  Reach Your Goal: Stretching & Mobility Exercises for Fitness, Personal Training, & Martial Arts .  Thanks to ARB for taking the excellent photos! Enter Corelight I have been working at Corelight since August 2018 . Our Corelight network security monitoring platform is really amazing and I suggest everyone check it out. We continue to have big plans for the future.  Zeek Communicator Since 2018 I have assumed the communications role for the Zeek network security monitoring project . Besides posting announcements to Mastodon  and LinkedIn , I also share interaction and admin duties for our Slack ,  Discourse , and YouTube sites. I'm working with the leadership team on strategies for growing community size and involvement in 2023 and beyond. Hobbies During the last five years, I earned a black belt equivalent in Krav Maga Global (the system uses patches, not belts) and a blue belt in Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu  (helping me to survive grappling with Jeremiah Grossman at the 2019 BJJ Smackdown during Black Hat ). I've retired from practicing martial arts , for now at least. However, my Martial History Team project continues, with plans through June 2025. I read a ton of books every month, but almost none have to do with technical security topics. My interests include US Civil War history, general military and nation state strategy, unidentified aerial phenomena, airpower, science, intelligence, and other topics. I have a strict monthly schedule and thus far have been able to stick to it for the last 16 months. I don't write reviews anymore, but I do write surveys for the martial arts books -- 36 so far. Finally, in 2022 I returned to one of my childhood hobbies, first begin in the fall of 1982 -- tabletop roleplaying games. I've been informally studying science fiction RPGs since the beginning of last year, potentially to begin another PhD program. I think it would be interesting to research a history PhD involving science fiction RPGs. I don't say much publicly about this, although I do have a Mastodon account for Science Fiction TTRPGs . I've also been playing in an online Star Frontiers campaign with a group scattered throughout the US.  SF was the first RPG I ever played, so it was cool to return to playing on its 40th birthday in August 2022. Conclusion As you might discern, I'm expressing myself in many different venues. As a result, I don't feel the need or desire to post here, at least not that often. In 2003, most of the platforms mentioned in this post didn't exist. Blogs were the hot new communication medium. Prior to that, security people published "white papers" in text form to sites like Packet Storm ! (Check out two of my entries here . Those are the PDF versions.) As far as security goes, I mostly care about the operational/campaign and higher levels of conflict, e.g.: In my opinion, the tactics used by intruders and defenders, and even most of the tools, have not really changed in the last 10 years, and definitely not since 2018. The operations/campaigns and strategies used by both sides haven't really changed either.  There are a few exceptions, like the massive SolarWinds supply chain compromise Mandiant discovered and published in December 2020 . Ransomware has definitely ramped up to gross levels since 2018. However, there haven't been any game-changers as far as how offense and defense interact.  Sure, way more processing is done in the cloud, and just about everything is running a vulnerable computer. However, no one on the offensive or defensive sides has significantly innovated to alter the way the two parties interact. Until that changes, security for me is largely a less interesting, but still unsolved, wicked problem.  Thank you to everyone who has been part of this blog's journey since 2003! Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

Until that changes, security for me is largely a less interesting, but still unsolved, wicked problem.  Thank you to everyone who has been part of this blog's journey since 2003! title: The Humble Hub
summary: Over the weekend I organized some old computing equipment. I found this beauty in one of my boxes. It's a Netgear EN104TP hub . I've mentioned this device before, in this blog and my books. This sort of device was the last of the true hubs. In an age where cables seem reserved for data centers or industrial facilities, and wireless rules the home and office, this hub is a relic of days gone past. To give you a sense of how old this device is, the Netgear documentation (still online -- well done) offers a PDF created in August 1998. (Again, well done Netgear, not mucking about with the timestamps.) I'm not sure how old my specific device is. Seeing as I started working in the AFCERT in the fall of 1998, I could see this hub being easily over 20 years old.  A hub is a network device that accepts traffic from its ports and repeats the traffic to all other ports. This is different from a switch, which maintains a table identifying which MAC addresses are in use on which ports. Before building this CAM (content addressable memory, IIRC) table, traffic to a new previously unforeseen MAC address will appear on all ports save the sender. This is a "true hub" because all of the ports are 10 Mbps. Yes, that is 100 times "slower" than the Gigabit ports on modern devices, if they have Ethernet ports at all. Starting with 10/100 Mbps devices, they all became switches. I never encountered a 100 Mbps "hub." Every device I ever had hands on was a 10/100 Mbps switch. That meant you were unlikely to see traffic on all ports when using a 10/100 Mbps device or even a 100 Mbps device (which I never saw anyway). There were no Gigabit (1000 Mbps) hubs built. I don't think the specification even supports it. These little boxes were network monitoring enablers. If you wanted to learn, or troubleshoot, or possibly even add monitoring to a production network, you could connect an upstream cable, a downstream cable, and a monitoring cable to the hub. The upstream could be a router and the downstream might be a firewall, and the monitoring would be your NSM server. If you were looking at traffic between two individual computers and needed visibility for a NSM laptop, you would plug all three into the hub, and plug your Internet upstream into the fourth port. I haven't needed this device in years, but I plan to keep it as a physical artifact of a time long past. At least this one still powers on, unlike my first computer, a Timex Sinclair ZX-80. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: Zeek in Action Videos
summary: This is a quick note to point blog readers to my Zeek in Action YouTube video series for the Zeek network security monitoring project .  Each video addresses a topic that I think might be of interest to people trying to understand their network using Zeek and adjacent tools and approaches, like Suricata, Wireshark, and so on.  I am especially pleased with Video 6 on monitoring wireless networks . It took me several weeks to research material for this video. I had to buy new hardware and experiment with a Linux distro that I had not used before -- Parrot .  Please like and subscribe, and let me know if there is a topic you think might make a good video. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: New Book! The Best of TaoSecurity Blog, Volume 4
summary: I've completed the TaoSecurity Blog book series . The new book is  The Best of TaoSecurity Blog, Volume 4: Beyond the Blog with Articles, Testimony, and Scholarship .  It's available now for Kindle , and I'm working on the print edition.  I'm running a 50% off promo on Volumes 1-3 on Kindle through midnight 20 April. Take advantage before the prices go back up. I described the new title thus: Go beyond TaoSecurity Blog with this new volume from author Richard Bejtlich. In the first three volumes of the series, Mr. Bejtlich selected and republished the very best entries from 18 years of writing and over 18 million blog views, along with commentaries and additional material.  In this title, Mr. Bejtlich collects material that has not been published elsewhere, including articles that are no longer available or are stored in assorted digital or physical archives. Volume 4 offers early white papers that Mr. Bejtlich wrote as a network defender, either for technical or policy audiences. It features posts from other blogs or news outlets, as well as some of his written testimony from eleven Congressional hearings. For the first time, Mr. Bejtlich publishes documents that he wrote as part of his abandoned war studies PhD program. This last batch of content was only available to his advisor, Dr. Thomas Rid, and his review committee at King's College London. Read how the security industry, defensive methodologies, and strategies to improve national security have evolved in this new book, written by one of the authors who has seen it all and survived to blog about it. This will likely be my final collection of writings. I've discovered some documents that may be of interest to historians, so I may contribute those to a national security archive like my friend Jay Healey did a few years ago . The only other work I might do for these four volumes is to record Audible editions. That would take a while, but I'm thinking about it. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: The Origins of the Names TaoSecurity and the Unit Formerly Known as TAO
summary: What are the origins of the names TaoSecurity and the unit formerly known as TAO?  Introduction I've been reading Nicole Perlroth's new book This Is How They Tell Me the World Ends . Her discussion of the group formerly known as Tailored Access Operations, or TAO, reminded me of a controversy that arose in the 2000s. I had heard through back channels that some members of that group were upset that I was operating using the name TaoSecurity. In the 2000s and early 2010s I taught classes under the TaoSecurity brand, and even ran TaoSecurity as a single-person consultancy from 2005-2007.  The purpose of this post is to explain why, how, and when I chose the TaoSecurity identity, and to show that it is contemporaneous with the formal naming of the TAO group. The most reliable accounts indicate TaoSecurity predates the TAO brand. TaoSecurity Began with Kung Fu and Taoism With Sifu Michael Macaris, 21 June 1996 In the summer of 1994, after graduating from the Air Force Academy and before beginning my graduate program at what is now called the Harvard Kennedy School, I started watching re-runs of the 1970s David Carradine Kung Fu TV series, created by Ed Spielman . I was so motivated by the philosophical message of the program that I joined a kung fu school in Massachusetts. I trained there for two years, and studied what I could about Chinese history and culture. I learned from the show and that it was based on Taoism ( for example ) so I bought a copy of the Tao Te Ching by Lao Tzu and devoured it.  Visiting China Tai Chi on the Yangtze, May 1999 In the spring of 1999 my wife and I took a three week trip to China for our honeymoon. We were both interested in Chinese culture so it seemed like a great opportunity. It was an amazing trip, despite the fact that we were in China when the United States bombed the Chinese embassy in Belgrade .  I include these details to show that I was quite the fan of Chinese culture, well before any formal cyber threat intelligence reports associated me with China. I read books on Taoism and embraced its concepts. Creating TaoSecurity WHOIS lookup for taosecurity.com In the summer of 2000 I was a captain at the Air Force Computer Emergency Response Team, within the 33rd Information Operations Squadron. I decided I wanted to try creating a Web presence, so I registered the TaoSecurity domain name on 4 July 2000. The WHOIS record above shows 3 July, which is odd, because a previous post on the topic captured the correct date of 4 July 2000 . I also coined the phrase "the way of digital security." My wife commissioned an artist to design the TaoSecurity logo, which I have used continuously since then. At the time I had never heard of TAO. There was a good reason for that. TAO was just being born as well. General Hayden on Creating TAO Playing to the Edge by General Michael Hayden The first public source on the history of TAO appeared in a 2013 story for Foreign Policy by Matthew M. Aid . He claimed that the agency created TAO in 1997. While it is possible that members of what would later be named TAO were working a similar mission in 1997, his story requires details that I add next. A succinct source on the origins of the unit previously known as the TAO is the 18 October 2018 article by Steven Loleski. He wrote a piece called  From cold to cyber warriors: the origins and expansion of NSA’s Tailored Access Operations (TAO) to Shadow Brokers  ( PDF ). Mr. Loleski cited General Michael Hayden's 2016 book Playing to the Edge , which I quote more extensively here: " In the last days of 2000 , as we were rewiring the entire agency’s organizational chart (see chapter 2), we set up an enterprise called TAO, Tailored Access Operations, in the newly formed SIGINT Directorate (SID). We had toyed with some boutique end-point efforts before, but this was different. This was going to be industrial strength...And, even in a period of generalized growth, TAO became the fastest-growing part of NSA post-9/11, bar none." Seeing as General Hayden was in charge of NSA at the time, that would seem to make it clear that TaoSecurity preceded TAO by several months, at least. I also looked for details in the 2016 book  Dark Territory: The Secret History of Cyber War by Fred Kaplan. I've enjoyed several of his previous books, and he interviewed and cited me for the text. Mr. Kaplan explained how General Michael Hayden, NSA director from March 1999 to April 2005 , named the unit, as part of a general reorganization effort. Thanks to Cryptome and FOIA requests by Inside Defense   we can read the October 1999 report recommending organizational changes. That reorganization was the genesis for creating TAO. Kaplan on Creating TAO External Team Report Recommended Organization, 22 October 1999, Cryptome This document, titled EXTERNAL TEAM REPORT: A Management Review for the Director, NSA, October 22, 1999  mentions the need to reorganize the "Signals Intelligence Mission (SIM)" into "three offices, Global Response, Tailored Access and Global Network." The October 2000 public news story by Inside Defense about the reorganization  implies that it did not happen overnight.  Mr. Kaplan notes that General Hayden initiated his "One Hundred Days of Change" program on 15 November 1999. A three-day server crash in January 2000 hampered reform efforts, prompting big changes in NSA approaches to computing. However, TAO was eventually operating some time in 2000. Mr. Kaplan notes the following in his book: "It began, even under his expansion, as a small outfit: a few dozen computer programmers who had to pass an absurdly difficult exam to get in. The organization soon grew into an elite corps as secretive and walled off from the rest of the NSA as the NSA was from the rest of the defense establishment. Located in a separate wing of Fort Meade, it was the subject of whispered rumors, but little solid knowledge, even among those with otherwise high security clearances... Early on, TAO hacked into computers in fairly simple ways: phishing for passwords (one such program tried out every word in the dictionary, along with variations and numbers, in a fraction of a second) or sending emails with alluring attachments, which would download malware when opened.  Once, some analysts from the Pentagon’s Joint Task Force-Computer Network Operations were invited to Fort Meade for a look at TAO’s bag of tricks. The analysts laughed: this wasn’t much different from the software they’d seen at the latest DEF CON Hacking Conference; some of it seemed to be repackaged versions of the same software. Gradually, though, the TAO teams sharpened their skills and their arsenal." It's clear from this passage that TAO started as a small unit that conducted less exotic operations. It was difficult to join, but a far cry from the powerhouse it would soon become. It's also clear that knowledge of this organization was tightly controlled. Even the term "tailored access" was not associated publicly with NSA until the October 2000 reporting by Inside Defense, reproduced by Cryptome. Minihan's Role Dark Territory  by Fred Kaplan Circling back to the mention of 1997 in Mr. Aid's article, we do find the following in Mr. Kaplan's reporting: "Fort Meade’s would be the third box on the new SIGINT organizational chart—“tailored access.” [Lt Gen Kenneth] Minihan [NSA director 1996-1999] had coined the phrase. During his tenure as director, he pooled a couple dozen of the most creative SIGINT operators into their own corner on the main floor and gave them that mission. What CIA black-bag operatives had long been doing in the physical world, the tailored access crew would now do in cyberspace, sometimes in tandem with the black-baggers, if the latter were needed—as they had been in Belgrade—to install some device on a crucial piece of hardware. The setup transformed the concept of signals intelligence, the NSA’s stock in trade. SIGINT had long been defined as passively collecting stray electrons in the ether; now, it would also involve actively breaking and entering into digital machines and networks. Minihan had wanted to expand the tailored access shop into an A Group of the digital era, but he ran out of time. When Hayden launched his reorganization, he took the baton and turned it into a distinct, elite organization—the Office of Tailored Access Operations, or TAO." This reporting indicates that there was a tailored access group operating at NSA prior to General Hayden, but it was not actually named "TAO" and was not as large or exotic as what was to come. Conclusion "Tao inside," TAO's play on the Intel Inside marketing campaign To summarize, General Hayden assigned the name TAO to a group inside NSA in late 2000, months after I registered the TaoSecurity domain name. Although General Minihan had created a tailored access group during his tenure, the existence of that team, as well as what was later formally called TAO, was a close-held secret. The term "tailored access" did not appear in the public until Inside Defense's reporting of October 2000.  Although I worked in the unit (Air Intelligence Agency) that served as the cryptologic service group for NSA (the Air Force contribution to the agency), I was not aware of any tailored access teams when I chose TaoSecurity as the name for my repository of security ideas. I selected TaoSecurity to reflect my interest in Taoism, and it had nothing to do with TAO or the NSA. Copyright 2003-2020 Richard Bejtlich and TaoSecurity (taosecurity.blogspot.com and www.taosecurity.com)

title: AI and Security Research
summary: How secure are your agents? Does vibe coding really work? How can I get started? What’s an agent and how do I create one? What you still need to know if you’re vibe coding. 🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/ I’ve published this before but I’m now blogging over at Substack. Here are some of my recent stories. Stories are free for first 30 days. Subscribe and follow the sections that interest you. What I’ve vibe coded in the past 2.5 weeks that I couldn’t get done previously in like 4 years. A multi-agent work flow including security considerations, running things sequentially or in parallel KISS — Keep It Simple Silly — to improve AI coding results Securing custom agents — lots of considerations I’m currently working on an updated version of kicking off batch jobs with MFA — or more specifically, a Yubikey. Currently my two factors are IP address and a Yubikey but I’ll probably add a password into the mix later. I showed how to set up a secure pentest environment for Burp Collaborator I create a script for finding rogue TLS traffic based on Certificate Fingerprints. There’s a post on spotting a Man In The Middle Attack on AWS which is not always going to show up in VPC flow logs… I have a section for those who are less technical now called Cybersecurity Me, or anyone trying ot improve personal cybersecurity. One day I logged into my Fidelity account and CoPilot was connected — what? I have never used CoPilot. Beware of what OpenClaw can do if you use it … Got tired of phone scammers on my phone — here’s how you can report them Does your bank support Yubikeys? They should. Not Passkeys. Yubikeys. Wrote some posts on security bugs. Expect more in this category soon. Took a look at a “vulnerability” in Amazon Bedrock when creating agents in a sandbox I showed how to find security bugs with AI while coding Took a look at which programming languages are best at preventing serialization bugs that lead to vulnerabilities Where are the crytographic bits stored when you register a FIDO compliant passkey — it depends I create a script that monitors network traffic including showing you the executables that are generating that traffic Took a look at what caused the Lexus Nexus Breach Dove into an authentication architecture and things you should be asking your vendors about that I have links to all my class materials over there. May migrate everything to that platform in the future. Then there’s my link to get the code on GitHub currently…which may move in the future. If you are just getting started with AI coding here’s how: Implement A Simple Project With Kiro CLI and a README.md Subscribe at this link and follow the sections that interest you most. teriradichel.substack.com — Teri Radichel

Here are some of my recent stories. Stories are free for first 30 days. Subscribe and follow the sections that interest you. What I’ve vibe coded in the past 2.5 weeks that I couldn’t get done previously in like 4 years. A multi-agent work flow including security considerations, running things sequentially or in parallel KISS — Keep It Simple Silly — to improve AI coding results Securing custom agents — lots of considerations I’m currently working on an updated version of kicking off batch jobs with MFA — or more specifically, a Yubikey. Currently my two factors are IP address and a Yubikey but I’ll probably add a password into the mix later. I showed how to set up a secure pentest environment for Burp Collaborator I create a script for finding rogue TLS traffic based on Certificate Fingerprints. There’s a post on spotting a Man In The Middle Attack on AWS which is not always going to show up in VPC flow logs… I have a section for those who are less technical now called Cybersecurity Me, or anyone trying ot improve personal cybersecurity. title: 2.5 Weeks of Vibe Coding
summary: Compared to similar code I tried to implement for years to deploy an AWS environment for running batch jobs (as AI agents or not). How I did it. On March 7th , 2026 I published a post about creating a way to push a Yubikey to run a job . As always one thing led to another and here I am again, essentially trying to set up a common infrastructure to execute batch jobs (or if you must, AI agents, though not all my jobs use AI). In this post I’ll tell you what I have accomplished since that date as of today, March 24th in the wee hours of the morning. That’s 17 days later. About 2 and a half weeks of vibe coding . I have a reusable bootstrap script that deploys an AWS environment in an OU with multiple accounts and resources. Skip to the bottom of the post for the full list of resources. What I’ve Vibe Coded In 2.5 Weeks On March 7th, 2026 I published a post about creating a way to push a Yubikey to run a job. title: AI in a Lambda Function, Amazon Bedrock Agents, Cookie Cutter Vibe Coding, Finding Security Bugs…
summary: AI in a Lambda Function, Amazon Bedrock Agents, Cookie Cutter Vibe Coding, Finding Security Bugs with AI Stories this week published to the Substack blog — teriradichel.substack.com 🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/ I’m finding that Substack is not as good at SEO as Medium. If you don’t already have a following it may be easier for you to get started on Medium. But overall I’m liking the functionality that Substack has to offer. For those not following me over there yet here are some of the latest stories. I tried out AI in a Lambda function (Querying CloudTrail Logs and getting Recommendations to Fix Them), played around with an Amazon Bedrock agent, and showed how to create a simple AWS Kiro CLI project with a README.md file. I also explain how to create cookie cutter projects to save on tokens. AWS pushes steering docs for Kiro but I’m having decent success with a simple README.md file for my purposes. I might not be doing what everyone else is doing but for me this simple approach is working well so far. I also wrote about a number of strategies for using AI to find security bugs for those who are interested in such things. My lab code for classes is now on GitHub and I explain some of the things I attempted to do to minimize the risks associated with giving students access to cloud resources in classes. I’ve also got a link to my GitHub posts in the old-school looking CODE category. You can follow for free or subscribe and choose only the categories which interest you rather than everything if some of the topics do not pertain to you. title: Mission Accomplished — A Post in Every Security Section
summary: Mission Accomplished — A Post in Every Security Section (Except the get the code section coming later) Mission accomplished. I have a post in every section on the new blog. Except the last one where I’m going to hopefully publish actual code — that’s coming later for various reasons. One of the cool things on substack is that you can follow only the sections that interest you most. One of the sections is for less technical users and people who want to secure their own assets. I created that section because invariably I’m giving a presentation on something unrelated and people just want to know how to “secure their own stuff.” I also get questions from my Mom who says she has no idea what I’m talking about. I’m going to try to keep it simpler in that section and explain technical things hopefully in terms anyone can understand, but maybe just on the edge of getting technical for those who need and want that information. That section is called Cybersecure Me and I just wrote a post about Bank Accounts and Yubikeys which seems to be pretty popular. The other section I hadn’t yet published in is called Security Insight . This is a catch all category for people who work for organizations trying to reduce cybersecurity risk. I might analyze breaches or provide insights that help companies lower their risk by taking various actions. This category might also be interesting for those who invest in cybersecurity products. I just posted about Using AI to Analyze Network Packets which I think is going to be a game change for defenders working in SOCs. As a side note I gripe about products and companies spamming the Internet with packets in the name of security. Overall I think this blog is more representative of me at the current moment than my existing Medium blog. It hits the categories I want to talk about more directly and it targets the different groups of people who have different interests a bit better. We’ll see how it works out. Subscribe for free. Posts will end up behind a paywall after 30 days the way I currently have it configured. https://teriradichel.substack.com

title: Teri Radichel on Substack
summary: Pondering publishing on Substack — I did it… 🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/ I’m pondering publishing on Substack so if you are following here you might want to sign up on my Stubstack feed. Teri Radichel :: Security and Technology Research | Substack And now, I need your help. I would like to know if there is any interest in any of the following services. One of the things I’m pondering doing on Substack is a Q & A service where people can submit written questions and I will answer them if I know the answer or possibly research the answers or get help from other experts if I don’t. Is this something people would be willing to pay for? What are your questions? What if you could expense the subscription to your company? The founding tier is more expensive because that is the tier at which you can ask and I will answer questions. Other tiers can read the Q & A. I’m not sure I’m actually going to do this without some additional research. Let me know what information you need and are willing to pay for by signing up for my Substack feed and dropping me a line. Note that I won’t blatantly promote products I don’t use or spend a lot of time for a small amount of money doing a lot of legwork to research and market a product. I’m not an influencer. But if there was a substantial subscription from a company and its employees I would use and write about a product — honestly. I could try it out and write about my experience using it as I have written about many other products in this blog. I will not write exactly what a company tells me to write or deal with editors from the company other than basic spelling and grammar. I have not had good experiences with a lot of editors (except the editor for my book — who was amazing.) Not a fan of people rewriting my work to say something other than I intended to say. What about materials for educators teaching classes? Do you need help? Anyway, I’m not sure what I’m going to do just yet. I’ll keep you posted here. But sign up on Substack for both free and paid posts and let me know if you have any interest in any particular paid content. I like Medium, but the problem is I make absolutely no money here so I cannot justify a ton of time. That’s why my posts have dropped off a bit. I am also hesitant to share proprietary research I’m working on through my business but with paid subscriptions — maybe? I wish there was a way to also allow subscriptions on GitHub and I could write and share code more easily. Pondering ideas. Drop me a line on LinkedIn if you have any particular needs you’d be willing to pay for that would be worth my time to set up over there and I’ll do it. If not, I’ll just keep posting here or there infrequently. Or both. Follow here — and on Substack — for more. title: So You Say You Want A House…
summary: Juggling security, technology, and house projects in 2025 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ 🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/ ⚙️ Security Automation (Pre-AI). The Code . ⚙️ AI Automation . The Code . 🔒 Related Stories: Cybersecurity | Penetration Tests 💻 Free Content on Jobs in Cybersecurity | ✉️ Sign up for the Email List ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ It’s that time of year again — the end of the year off-topic post about my reflections on another year gone by. This year was particularly challenging for many reasons and one of the primary ones was the continuing saga of our antique house. For all the people who haven’t purchased a house yet but want to, you might want to read this first. This image was generated by Nano Banana. It got a decent Savannah porch in there but the roof doesn’t look exactly right and the paint looks fuzzy instead of like it’s peeling. You can usually still tell images are generated by AI if you look closely. Most of the marketing photos I see on cloud sites definitely look AI-generated. Still, probably close enough to give you an idea what I’m going to write about. :) This is going to be a pretty long brain dump because there are different considerations for different types of houses. In addition, all of this has been…a lot…Just kind of getting it all out there so anyone else buying a home or trying to fix one can learn from my mistakes and experiences. This also explains what my focus will be, in part, in 2026 and how that intersects with my technical research and goals. The lakeside condo I’m on my third house. The first one was a condo actually. I like this particular part of town in Seattle after I got out of school from the University of Washington in Seattle. I had rented a place in Wedgewood with my last college roommates (I had a wide variety over the years) up on a hill in a nice neighborhood north of school. That was an ok place to live but I found myself drawn to Magnuson Park on Lake Washington most of the time. I always want to live by water. I explored the places I could live right near that park and ran across these condominiums called “The Sand Piper.” I liked them the best of the ones I saw in that area. I noticed that there were advertisements on the window of the manager’s office where people were renting the condos out but I couldn’t afford them. I figured well if they can put up notices that they were renting maybe I could put up a little sign saying I wanted to rent one and how much I could pay. The manager was a nice old guy and said OK. Almost immediately after putting up my little sign I got a call. There was a man who had moved out of his condo to take care of his sick mother. We worked out an agreement and I rented his place. I lived there for a few years and then at some point, I contacted him and asked him if I could buy it. Amazingly he said OK and I bought it without a realtor for a pretty low price. I had a $450 per month payment and the stupidest thing I ever did was sell that condo. I needed the money to keep running my business. Never. Do. That. I thought I was going to move and rent temporarily and do the same thing to buy the next place. But it never worked out. The dot bomb crash hit and one thing after another went wrong and I ended up bouncing around for a bit. Transient home hopping I went to San Diego for a while to play beach volleyball, Australia (Manly Beach), Chicago for a couple of weeks to help a family member, stayed with my parents for a bit since I hadn’t seen them in a while, and then back to Seattle where I stayed with a friend for a couple of weeks and then found an apartment — on the water next to Alki Beach. I stayed in that apartment for a while and I love it. It was a building built in 1937 with historic windows and doors and features. It has the hugest big window. You just can’t find those things everywhere. I thought I’d probably never move. I thought that I should buy a house and have some equity but I loved my little apartment, the location, and everything about it. Alki Beach Bungalow But then my ex-business partner became a real estate agent and he asked me if I wanted to look at houses. I wanted to help him out so I said OK I’ll look — but don’t expect me to buy anything. I didn’t want him to get his hopes up. Literally on our first trip out, I walked into this little house with a view of Alki Beach that looked like nothing special in the listing. I walked in, walked up to the 1940’s solid wood window trim and put my hands on either side of looking at the water. “I want it!” The house was cheap by all standards due to the 2008 crash. The owners were elderly folks who had moved out and their kids were selling. The first deal had fallen through and they wanted to unload it. The house was just out of my price range based on the loan for which I was approved. I put in an offer for exactly the amount I had — and they accepted it! I must admit I got so lucky with that house. I think I bought at the very bottom of the market. I had a 2.5% interest loan. The guy that owned it was a Boeing engineer and he and his wife took care of it very well. It was a solid find with no major structural issues. By the time I left I think I fixed everything that was in my original inspection report. It was a cute 944 square foot (1440 with unfinished basement I loved) with a view and a pool. The house came with a pool. I grew up with a pool. I didn’t want a pool. They are a lot of work. But oh well maybe if I have a pool I will have a lot of people over for pool parties. I had a couple of parties but mostly the pool didn’t get a ton of use while I lived there. It was only really usable three months of the year and it was mostly an expensive hassle. Over the years I had the sewer line inspected and they blew fiberglass in it before I resurfaced the pool. That also fixed it so rats wouldn’t keep trying to get at it due to the leaks. I replaced an oil furnace with a heat pump. I refinished the kitchen including a custom spice cabinet that better fit the space, modified a closet to become a custom server and media cabinet closet, and put in a TV that came out of the ceiling in a very oddly configured living room. I redid the fireplace installing a wood-burning insert. Remodeled the bathroom with a custom designed vanity and shower. Changed all the lights. And painted it many, many times because I loved changing the colors. Oh and a lot of landscaping. OK so that house was pretty cool. So why did I move? Well after a while I just wanted to get out of Seattle for various reasons. It’s beautiful in some ways, in others not so much. I was looking around for where to move but in the end I didn’t know where to go so I just decided to stay. Then I started talking to my now husband. Long story short, I’m in Savannah and I love it. A Savannah Victorian Antique When I started looking at houses in Savannah, the only ones I truly liked were the historic houses. I LOVE historic houses. My house in Alki Beach was built in 1940’s which is not that old but for Seattle it’s older. At first I saw the old wood wall and thought — that has got to go. Then I read about the history of the wood walls and realized it wasn’t paneling. It was actually real wood. I left it. In that house I went for a mix of modern and historic but in Savannah I could get a truly antique house (over 100 years old). Those are few and far between in Seattle. In Savannah, there are still many 100+ year old houses. Savannah is a bit strange because you have a real mix of incomes and homes and city buildings all in the same neighborhood and sometimes on the same street or even block. It’s also expanding. The core historic homes go for millions of dollars in downtown Savannah. As you move outwards the homes become more run down in some areas and less historic in others. You can see how over the decades more and more homes were added, expanding the city outward. There are some neighborhoods with super nice homes outside of the historic district in Savannah in a particular area built in the 40’s and 50’s with somewhat questionable history. Well, really a lot of Savannah has such history. We were told that the condo kitty corner to us was a brothel, for instance. And this used to be one of the most dangerous parts of the city apparently. It’s changed over the years as with many neighborhoods — for better or for worse. When I moved here I could see the edge of the truly historic and the renovation moving outwards, north and south and west of the city. I found a location that was just on the edge of the “expensive” homes on the edge of a lot of construction and renovation and bought there. I looked at homes that had been renovated but I purposely found a historic home that had some issues so I could renovate it the way I wanted. The home I purchased has old fashioned wooden floors, brick chimneys, four fireplaces, a trunk room, a hallway that I presume used to be a butler’s pantry, huge pocket doors (I mean really huge), 12 foot ceilings, amazing trim, and truly historic windows (they still have ropes and chains, wavy glass , and historic wood and one huge bay window). The siding looks historic not like cement board and has the appropriate narrow sizing. I can always tell when windows and siding have been replaced with less historic materials. The columns are round, not just square beams added later after the original porch failed. But little did I know, the history and ability to renovate comes with a price. The luck I had with my first two homes did not transfer to this latest purchase. Yes, I read the inspection report. I knew it had some issues just like the other home I purchased. What I didn’t realize was the gravity of those issues and how they might impact the stability of home. Live and learn. I had to buy the house pretty fast because the market was so crazy. I did have an inspection and I did have a contractor come out and estimate the cost of replacing one failing beam. But what I didn’t realize was that one failing beam wasn’t the only or biggest problem. So the rest of this post is just a few tips on things I’ve learned — not only with this house but from all the places I have lived. HOA dues — know what you’re getting yourself into When you buy a home in some places — most condos, townhomes, gated communities and what are called “plantations” here in the south — come with HOA dues. HOA dues are for the homeowners association associated with the location and I’m not going to get into ALL the drama you can face being part of a location that has HOA dues. There are pros and cons to HOA dues. The pros — they take care of a lot of things for you like landscaping and some maintenance external to your property. The cons — you have to pay a monthly fee that may be raised or worse — something big fails on the property and everyone has to chip in to fix it whether you have the money or not and whether you agree with the change or not. You likely have a vote but if your vote is in the minority you may end up having to come up with thousands of dollars to pay for something you may or may not want. I was clueless and got really lucky when I bought my condo at the Sand Piper because the HOA dues were low and they had just fixed the roof. I’ve known people who had to pay for fixing roofs, windows, and even pay for renovations in a shared restaurant and community center on the property that they never use. The inspection report At my first home, I had my dad come and inspect it. He built his own home and is very knowledgeable. I added his approval as a contingency in my real estate agreement. He inspected the house and gave his thumbs up — and he was right. That home was really solid and very well maintained. Most of the things I improved upon at that home were cosmetic. Now let’s talk about my home in Savannah. Yes, I got the inspection report. Yes, I read it. Yes I knew there were issues. But how everything transpired was not aligned with my expectations — exacerbated by the rise of interest rates and hiring some contractors that made it worse by ripping my house apart so I could not refinance as the interest rates skyrocketed. Live and learn. I made a good amount of money when I sold my Seattle home. I actually bought a cheaper home in Savannah so I could use the extra money to fix it up. It’s amazing how fast that money evaporated. That’s what it feels like anyway. Taxes I had to pay more taxes than expected from the sale due to a mistake I made after moving. I should have stayed in Seattle and not changed my mailing address until the deal was complete. I had a Georgia address while transitioning and ended up having to pay Georgia state tax on my home sale even though I hadn’t officially become a Georgia resident yet! Interest rates and bank shenanigans I also didn’t secure a loan due to weird issues with the bank that financed the loan — and approved me and then UN-approved me when I had hundreds of thousands of dollars sitting in the bank. To this day I am skeptical. I think someone at that bank may have wanted to buy my house because they saw it was a good deal. I ended up cashing out a bunch of stocks and paying in cash, but along with that came a big tax bill and no loan secured at a cheap interest rate. And about that good deal — I have issues to fix but the value of the house rose like $300K in two years so…am I right? Who knows if the market will hold but Savannah seems to be expanding in a good way, even if it has some problems. I am still happy with my purchase even in light of some of the issues I’m about to tell you about. Ok so now about that inspection report… Electric bills My house in Savannah is four times bigger than my house in Seattle. Holy electric bills. Savannah has some times where the weather is mild and we turn off the air conditioner and heating system, but in those summer months…phew. And there are about two months in the winter where it gets very cold, rainy and we had some unusually cold freezes. Beyond that I learned that the back half of the house is missing a lot of insulation …and eventually I realized that the back of the house was literally peeling off the rest of the house due to a failing foundation and maybe pressure from above. Hold that thought. We are literally heating and cooling the city of Savannah. I’m talking $600–700 per month in some cases. Insane! I ended up plugging a bunch of things as best I could and we put some stupid looking blankets up on the door to our pantry and laundry room — but there’s literally a gap above both those door frames where they are breaking away from the house and the air is seeping in. I don’t think that was in our inspection report. HVAC Ducts Our inspection report mentioned some issues with the ducts under the house. Maybe chewed up by animals. Ok. More on that later. What I learned was that those ducts may be contributing to the expense of heating and cooling the house. So of course I got those fixed almost right away. While I was at it I had them install a sump pump kind of thing to deal with water under the house. The HVAC guy told me there was some strange pipe going all the way from one side of the house to the other under the house. There was no apparent reason at the time as I hadn’t lived in the house long enough to know why. It would be cheaper just to have the water leak out to the closer side. Ok sure. But then I saw that the water was just draining out on the side of the house and not going anywhere except probably back under the house. The HVAC guy shrugged. Not his thing. So what’s the point of a sump pump that drains to nowhere? That makes no sense. I had some challenges with the whole drainage problem so let’s talk about that. Water So much water. The first two times I came to Savannah it was sunny and nice — May and November. Then I came in December and I told my husband that I thought he had pulled one over on me. Rain in Georgia doesn’t play. When it rains, the sky opens up and the clouds dump over barrels of rain all at once. The thunder is earth shattering. Sometimes it shakes the whole house. The inspection report said there was some standing water under our house. Yeah yeah ok. No really, there ended up being a river flowing between our house and the neighbor’s house. Not only that the city sewer line in front of our house had issues. I joked that when it rains we had lake front property. That is not uncommon in Savannah apparently. Some roads are worse than others. Hold that thought. Pondering the foundation OK so there’s some issues with the bricks in the pilings holding the house up in the inspection report. Ok sure. I know the floors aren’t level and seems like that all needs to be fixed at once. That’s logical right? I called Ram Jack and another similar company for a quote and they were either $17K or $45K. I had no idea it was going to be so much after the things I had fixed in Seattle to fix some bricks under the house. Then a structural engineer told me I probably didn’t need that. I could just put car jacks down there yada yada. The floors can be leveled from inside the house. Ok. (But how do you maintain the antique floor…? More on that in a minute.) The gist of it was that Ram Jack, that company that is always on TV might be overkill. I held off at that moment to explore other options. I hired a different structural engineer ( Brian Sapp of Sapp Structural Engineering and Inspections ) because the first one disappeared and he gave me a second opinion on everything. He gave me some temporary fixes until we would were ready to do the big project. Would they last? Who knows. Only time would tell. I went seeking for someone to help me put in those temp fixes while we waited for interest rates to come down. The guest bathroom I hired a contractor and that’s a whole long story I won’t bore you with completely. I wanted him to finish this temp project and help come up with a plan for the larger project. After waiting for a year for him at the recommendation of some others he took over six months to complete a project that he suggested could be done in 3–4 weeks. One thing I wanted him to do was just replace some moldy panels at the bottom of our guest bathroom wall and replace them with matching panels from the laundry room. This was a temporary fix because I had guests coming over and the moldy walls were gross. Instead his carpenter ripped out the whole bathroom wall! My contractor also said he could not tell what to do with the back room until he ripped the ceiling off so I said go ahead. He removed and uh…yeah. Everything is tacked together in that ceiling the weirdest ways. There’s a hip roof over once side and an ugly modernish new roof on the other half. Underneath is a disaster. I just told him — we’re just going to rip that out and redo it. Somehow. THEN. I find out that the bank won’t give us a loan while the house is “under construction.” Do you think a contractor should know this when he knows we are trying to refinance to get a loan to do the project? That we should not do that until we secure the loan? I don’t know. Seems odd. So I ask him if he can quickly put the house back together. I also am pondering if I should just fix the bathroom wall or the whole bathroom. He claims he can probably get the bathroom done in three weeks. Yeah. Six months later interest rates are up over 2% or more and I ended up hiring my own plumber, my own electrician, and asking him to not bring the drywall people back. I and my nephew finished the drywall (mostly me in the end and I’m not great) just to get it done before we had guests over for my husband’s birthday. I even had to trick my husband into helping me and he hates tools. He was screwing in the plate covering the bathroom fan at the last minute while I scrambled to complete everything else (I didn’t) when the first guests walked through the door. Phew. I ended up doing some things to make that bathroom historic like restoring an antique chandelier I got off eBay, an antique side table I got at a local shop, art-deco-ish tiles, a historic looking sink, and a poster of a Life magazine cover from 1922. Our house was built in 1920. Now, before I had the contractor fix the bathroom he swore that there would be no reason why we shouldn’t do that. The foundation was solid. They could fix the adjoining rooms from within those rooms, etc. OK sure. We’ll see. It’s probably the nicest room in our house right now. Just give me a plan, ok? I need a plan. After that I gave up on refinancing as I realized I had jumped on a good (not great) rate at just the right time and secured it. Since then, even though the rates have dropped like three times, they are barely lower than that rate if they are at all. Those rates are killing our finances. I spent thousands and thousands of dollars more on interest thanks to our house getting ripped apart and not being able to refinance right at that moment. Also not being able to secure the initial home loan which would have been at 3.5%. Also another dumb mistake — I was going to get a fixed rate HELOC and our next local bank convinced me not to do that. Kicking myself. Great thanks. That and the cost of everything went up — building materials, groceries, and now with tariffs so much more is costing more than if we had been able to do the project immediately when I wanted to do it. I never should have waited for that original contractor. But I was new to the city and I know it’s a small town where everyone knows each other and I just wasn’t sure what to do. I wanted to be everybody’s friend but that cost us a lot of money. Initially while waiting on that contractor I was hoping he would help us come up with a plan for fixing the house. I contacted multiple architects and it was hard to find one. The first one wanted to project manage the whole project. I couldn’t find examples of his work anywhere and for whatever reason he would not give me a complete price, only a price for the “as-built.” I wasn’t even sure if he could do the entire plan based on what I could find and I didn’t want a project manager scheduling meetings with all these people who bill by the hour. I wasn’t ready to hire all those people. I just wanted drawings that showed what I wanted the house to look like and my plan for fixing some structural issues and THEN I would get the right contractor to implement the plan. Mind you, he was super nice and may have been awesome but I really needed to know a budget so I could ensure I had enough money in the bank to pay him. I have learned from past business problems not to start anything until the money is in the bank. And I didn’t want the project managed the way he was indicating. I didn’t need a project manager — though I’m sure he would be a great one if that’s what you need. My contractor recommended a couple of architects. They set up meetings with me but never gave me a contract or a price! Is everyone really just that busy or what is going on here? I heard that people sometimes just want the high end projects and so they disregard the smaller projects. So what’s the deal? We pay our bills just like anyone else. Give me a quote! Give me a contract! I can’t get any architect to give me a darn contract. Finally I found Josh Ward of Ward Architecture + Preservation . He’s amazing. He immediately sent me a contract but said he could not start right away. No problem. I paid a small very reasonable deposit. I wasn’t in a hurry at that point. I was extremely overloaded with work last year. Finally I had a bit of time and we had the money in the bank to pay him and his time freed up we got started. He is amazing, super nice, and his drawings were perfectly what I wanted with just a few tweaks. After completing that plan I was planning to get a contractor to help me implement the solution (using the terminology from the technical world in which I exist.) But then all hell broke loose… The big crack We didn’t quite finish the architectural plan when suddenly I go into my husband’s “man cave” next to the bathroom where I’ve been using the bathtub and I’m like, “Did you want to tell me the crack in the wall had expanded and is now HUGE?!” I’m freaking out. My husband never lived in a house that needed extensive repairs or had been remodeled. Things related to the house don’t phase him that much and most of the home repairs and planning is on me, which I am fine with. But I didn’t realize what was happening in that room because I never go in there. I grew up in remodels. I know a lot of the pitfalls. While my dad was trying to fix a ceiling it fell on his head — and he’s pretty particular and careful about how he does things. (His parents spoke German if you know what I mean.) He ended up building a church and his own house. Along the way he pulled all the trim in a historic house in Yelm, Washington used for a church parsonage, numbered it, fixed the walls, and then put all the trim back exactly as it was before he started. For anyone who says they need to rip out trim, windows, and other historic features, I know the truth. Yes it costs more. Yes it’s a pain. But I know it can be done. And if you’re going to buy an antique, why not restore it and maintain the antique features? Otherwise, what’s the point? Go buy a new house if that’s what you really want. Oh and if you live in the historic district in Savannah you are forced to do that by regulation. I am not in that neighborhood but I still want to maintain the historic qualities of our home. So anyway back to our beloved crack. The back of the house was clearly remodeled. When we ripped off the ceiling and part of the walls in the back room we found newspapers from 2008. The back of the house…is shoddy to say the least. We found out from a neighbor that at some point that part of the house caught on fire which likely is what led to the less than ideal repairs. I’ve always planned to rip a lot of it off and redo it while maintaining any historic features. But we weren’t quite ready to start. Well, now was the time. I frantically told the architect we need to hold off for a minute (well more than a minute) and he agreed. I called the structural engineer again with the idea that I needed a plan that worked with our pretty much complete architecture but apparently we had a miscommunication. He gave me another report. I have never worked with a structural engineer before so apparently I don’t use the correct words or whatever to communicate what I need because I wanted a solution — not another report. But it all worked out in the end and Brian was great. Drainage I wasn’t sure what to do at that point but remember that river flowing beside and under our house? And the HVAC system that we changed to push the water out the other side? Well, I figured that water might be exacerbating the problem under that wall so I searched around trying to find someone to deal with our drainage problem. This is not as easy as it sounds.
